ankai: Ankai has the highest retention risk of all relationships — HR must prepare backup plans
Kai-party candidate: 'nothing else is bearable' — output declines after six months, high probability of departure within one year
An-party candidate will be continuously drained by the team — appears stable but internally overdrawn
Bad year plus ankai means near-certain departure — consider contract arrangements to reduce risk
Initial excitement and high output mask underlying drain — don't be fooled by short-term performance
yusui: Sui-party candidate's output diminishes over time — first year is the peak, then declines annually
Yu-party candidate over-invests in energizing the team, but fatigue is hard to detect
Sui party 'should not relocate or travel' — drained to the point of wanting to leave but unable to, HR should evaluate proactively
Bad year plus sui party means double drain — peak turnover period
kisei: Ki-party candidate's stress tolerance has limits — beyond that threshold, growth becomes harm
The adjustment period may be longer than expected — HR shouldn't rush to judgment during probation
Initial friction doesn't mean poor fit — use a full three-to-six-month observation cycle to evaluate
mei: Same mansion lacks complementarity — high team homogenization risk
Candidate won't bring new perspectives — if the team is already homogeneous, additional same-type hiring is inappropriate
Culture fit is high but innovation capability is insufficient — evaluate what the team currently needs
gyotai: Gyo-party candidate tends to over-invest, neglecting personal needs — HR should monitor work-life balance
Natural rapport may lead managers to skip necessary communication and expectation setting — creates hidden long-term issues
